fact: The Empress of Ireland sank in fourteen minutes
source: Empress of Ireland: The Story of an Edwardian Liner, Derek Grout (2014); also widely documented

fact: A coal freighter (the SS Storstad) appeared out of fog and struck the Empress of Ireland broadside
source: Report of the Commission of Inquiry into the Loss of the Empress of Ireland, Lord Mersey (1914)

fact: Passengers slept with portholes open on a warm May night, and water poured through open portholes as the ship rolled
source: Forgotten Empress: The Empress of Ireland Story, David Zeni (1998); testimony from the Mersey Commission of Inquiry

fact: The river water was approximately four degrees Celsius
source: Canadian Museum of History — Empress of Ireland exhibit documentation; water temperature of the St. Lawrence in late May widely cited as ~4°C (33–39°F)

fact: 1,012 people died, most trapped inside the ship
source: Report of the Commission of Inquiry into the Loss of the Empress of Ireland (1914). Official death toll is 1,012 of 1,477 aboard.

fact: The ship carried enough lifeboats for all passengers and crew, but almost none were successfully launched
source: Forgotten Empress: The Empress of Ireland Story, David Zeni (1998); Commission of Inquiry testimony confirmed the ship met lifeboat regulations but the extreme list prevented deployment
